content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Non-structural compensatory reform (leave selection age unchanged)'. Because early selection is codified as a narrow, separately-justified legal exception rather than the structural default of secondary education, reform of the selection age is legally more tractable than reshaping Hungary's broader maintainer or curriculum architecture — but legitimacy and constitutional doctrine require any change to proceed on a felmenő-rendszer (grade-by-grade phase-in) basis with minimum ~1-year statutory lead time, protecting students already admitted under existing rules.
opportunities: Achievement gains in the range of Portugal's observed 0.3 SD improvement over roughly 15-18 years are plausible for the comprehensive (non-tracked) majority of Hungarian pupils if curriculum, autonomy, and assessment reforms are implemented with comparable fidelity.

Politically less disruptive than structural reform since it does not touch the statutory definition of secondary education, avoiding the felmenő-rendszer obligations and the direct Fidesz 'totalitarian unified model' framing risk that S3 faces — though this should not be read as evidence the package is politically risk-free, given that targeted funding for disadvantaged schools is independently a fiscal-consolidation target.
strengths: Hungarian law explicitly allows gymnasium (4, 6 or 8-grade forms) but treats the 6/8-year track as a distinct legal category requiring 'talent-nurturing' (tehetséggondozás) justification with separately regulated elevated standards, making selection age a codified exception rather than the structural default.

The Nkt. § 4 defines secondary education (középfokú nevelés-oktatás szakasza) as beginning at grade 9, with the 6- and 8-year gymnasium explicitly listed as the only statutory exception to this age boundary.
threats: Targeted TEIP-style funding lines are likely first-line cuts during fiscal consolidation (deficit projected at 6.2% in 2026, debt rising to 76.8% of GDP by 2027), making the central benefit-delivery mechanism budget-vulnerable in a way that significantly undermines the durability of any expected equity gain, even though the scenario avoids the structural-reform reversal risks facing S1-S3.

The scenario's political attractiveness as a 'lower-risk' alternative may itself become a political liability if framed by reform advocates or the European Commission as inadequate given Hungary's documented status as the EU's most socially selective system, risking accusations of policy avoidance rather than genuine reform.
weaknesses: Whether Hungary's Constitutional Court (Alkotmánybíróság) would treat abolition or significant raising of the 6/8-year selection age as a matter binding the felmenő-rendszer safeguard as mandatory constitutional doctrine, or whether it would classify it as a discretionary legislative choice subject only to rule-of-law process review.

Whether non-state maintainers (churches, foundations under remaining autonomy frameworks) would face differential legal constraints or political veto power in a national selection-age reform, and whether consensus-building with maintainer federations would constitute a hard prerequisite or a discretionary negotiation.
